chunk_summary: This section discusses dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation in India, emphasizing poor complementary feeding practices, their impact on child growth, and practical clinical approaches for improvement.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation in pediatric care.
Identify key challenges in complementary feeding practices in India based on NFHS-5 data.
Learn practical tools and structured approaches for evaluating and improving feeding practices.
Recognize the clinical significance of the first 1,000 days in child development.
key_facts: Only 23% of Indian children aged 6–23 months meet minimum dietary diversity (NFHS-5, 2019–21).
Just 11% of children in this age group achieve a minimum acceptable diet (NFHS-5, 2019–21).
The first 1,000 days are critical for growth, neurodevelopment, and illness recovery.
Common feeding errors include delayed complementary feeding, overly dilute feeds, and coercive feeding.
Practical tools for assessment include 24-hour recalls, food group screens, and direct feeding observations.
reference_quote: "Only about 23% of children 6–23 months receive minimum dietary diversity and about 11% achieve a minimum acceptable diet, contributing to growth faltering and micronutrient deficiencies." (NFHS-5, 2019–21)
document_key_topics: Challenges in complementary feeding in India
Practical tools for feeding skills evaluation
Structured clinical approaches for improving child nutrition
